verses.text: Dans ce chapitre, les généalogies des autres tribus d’Israël sont présentées. • Les sections portant sur les tribus d’Issacar , Benjamin , et Aser peuvent avoir été dérivées d’un recensement militaire ; les listes sont de longueur presque égale, mettent l’accent sur la terminologie militaire, enregistrent les maisons du père et ne donnent aucune information sur l’emplacement des campements. • Les tribus de Zabulon et Dan sont entièrement omises, et celle Nephthali est très brièvement présentée.\n\n

Le texte hébreu de ce verset semble avoir été altéré (quelque chose a apparemment été perdu lors de la copie par les scribes) car aucune introduction n’est donnée ni pour Ir ni pour Huschim. Les noms Schuppim et Huppim apparaissent dans la tribu de Benjamin ( **8.8** , **11** ; **Gn 46.21** ; **Nb 26.39** ). Si le texte hébreu a effectivement disparu partiellement, Huschim pourrait être un descendant de Dan (cf. **Gn 46.23** ; **Nb 26.42** ).

Contrairement à d’autres généalogies, cette liste abrupte des fils de Nephthali inclut seulement la première génération (cf. **Gn 46.24** ; **Nb 26.48-50** ). Le manuscrit des Chroniques peut avoir été partiellement endommagé au début de la copie par les scribes, ce qui expliquerait également l’omission de Dan et Zabulon. Il est aussi possible que les archives pour Zabulon, Dan et Nephthali aient été perdues lorsque Teglath-Phalasar a attaqué et exilé ces tribus pendant le règne de Pékach ( **2R 15.29** ) ; dans ce cas, le Chroniqueur n’avait pas de documents disponibles à inclure.

Plusieurs difficultés dans ces versets semblent indiquer que le texte hébreu a été endommagé : (1) Maaca est mentionnée comme la sœur de Makir ainsi que comme sa femme ( **7.15-16** ) ; (2) Huppim et Schuppim ont été mentionnés plus tôt avec Benjamin ( **7.12** ), pas avec Manassé ; (3) ceux considérés comme Galaadites ( **7.16-17** ) sont plutôt liés de manière ambiguë à Galaad, dont les descendants directs ne sont pas mentionnés (cf. **Nb 26.30-33** ) ; (4) les fils de Schemida ne sont pas reliés à la généalogie ( **1Ch 7.19** ) ; Schemida était l’un des descendants de Galaad ( **Nb 26.32** ).\n\n

La généalogie des descendants d’Éphraïm inclut une histoire illustrant les circonstances de l’installation de la tribu en Canaan ( **7.21-24** ). La généalogie se termine avec Josué, fils de Nun , qui a conduit Israël dans la Terre Promise.\n\n

La généalogie des descendants d’Éphraïm inclut une histoire illustrant les circonstances de l’installation de la tribu en Canaan ( **7.21-24** ). La généalogie se termine avec Josué, fils de Nun , qui a conduit Israël dans la Terre Promise.\n\n

Le père d’Ézer et d’Élead est mentionné comme étant Éphraïm , mais Éphraïm, le fils de Joseph, n’aurait pas pu s’installer en Canaan après l’Exode. La solution traditionnelle consistait à imaginer un exode précoce pour la famille d’Éphraïm. Une autre possibilité est que le nom Éphraïm soit une erreur de scribe ou que Éphraïm désigne la tribu plutôt que le fils de Joseph portant ce nom.

7:23 And Heber,.... The other grandson of Asher; and son of Beriah: begat Japhlet, and Shomer, and Hotham, and Shuah their sister; a place on the borders of Ephraim is called the coast of Japhleti; but whether from this Japhlet is uncertain.

7:24 And the sons of Japhlet; Pasach, and Bimhal, and Ashvath; these are the children of Japhlet. Of whom we read not elsewhere, And the sons of Japhlet; Pasach, and Bimhal, and Ashvath; these are the children of Japhlet. Of whom we read not elsewhere, 1 Chronicles 7:34 ch1 7:34 ch1 7:34 ch1 7:34And the sons of Shamer,.... Or Shomer, the brother of Japhlet, 1 Chr 7:32. Ahi, and Rohgah, Jehubbah, and Aram; of whom nothing is known but their names.

7:25 And the sons of his brother Helem,.... Or Helem his brother, that is, the brother of Shomer, who, according to Hillerus (n), is Hotham, 1 Chr 7:32. Zophah, and Imna, and Shelesh, and Aram; nowhere else mentioned. (n) Onomastic. Sacr. p. 551.

7:26 Laadan his son, Ammihud his son, Elishama his son: The Ephraimite line that produced Joshua (7:27). Ammihud and Elishama are named as Joshua's grandfather and father; in Num 1:10 Elishama is chief of Ephraim's host at the second-year census, supplying the leader at the conquest.

7:27 Non his son, Jehoshua his son: *Jehoshua* (Joshua) — the Ephraimite who succeeded Moses and led the conquest — descends from a deliberately recorded line. The Chronicler closes the Ephraimite genealogy with Joshua, whose name signifies *YHWH saves* and looks forward to a greater Jeshua (Matt 1:21).
7:28 And the sons of Zophah,.... The eldest of the sons of Helem: Suah, and Harnepher, and Shual, and Beri, and Imrah, Bezer; and Hod, and Shamma, and Shilshah, and Ithran, and Beera; in all eleven.

7:29 And the sons of Jether,.... The same with Ithran, the last of Zophah's sons but one, 1 Chr 7:37, Jephunneh, and Pispah, and Ara; not Jephunneh the father of Caleb; he was not of the tribe of Asher, but of Judah.

7:30 And the sons of Ulla,.... Who either was the son of Ara, last mentioned, or another son of Jether: Arah, and Haniel, and Rezia; here ends the genealogy of Asher; the last of the tribes; Dan and Zebulun not being reckoned at all.

7:31 The sons of Beriah; Heber, and Malchiel, who is the father of Birzavith: The line of Asher continues. Malchiel founded Birzavith, an Asherite town; Heber would father Japhlet (7:32), whose descendants populate verses 33-34. The Chronicler tracks Asher's territorial roots in western Galilee.

7:32 All these were the children of Asher, heads of their father's house,.... Principal men in their tribe, and respective families: choice and mighty men of valour; these were some selected from others, being eminent for their courage and valour: chief of the princes; or chief princes; the Vulgate Latin version is, dukes of dukes, they were heads of their fathers' families: and the number throughout the genealogy that were apt to war, and to battle, was twenty and six thousand men;

7:33 The sons of Japhlet; Pasach, and Bimhal, and Ashvath: Three more Asherite names. Japhlet appears outside this genealogy only in Josh 16:3, naming a border town in Ephraim — the same family may have spread to Ephraimite territory, reflecting tribal intermingling along Israel's central spine.

7:34 The sons of Shamer; Ahi, and Rohgah, Jehubbah, and Aram: A second Asherite branch. *Aram* here is a personal name within Asher, not the northern kingdom — common-name collisions are typical in tribal lists and remind the reader that genealogy was lived as ordinary family memory.

7:35 The sons of his brother Helem; Zophah, and Imna, and Shelesh, and Amal: Helem's branch parallels Shamer's. The Asherite list grows by careful subdivision — each grandson named, each clan branch identified — preserving the territorial claims that mattered for inheritance and military census.

7:36 The sons of Zophah; Suah, and Harnepher, and Shual, and Beri, and Imrah: Zophah's six sons (continuing into 7:37) make him the most prolific branch in this section of Asher. The genealogy reads almost as a tribal directory — names that meant little to outsiders meant everything to the families they identified.

7:37 Bezer, and Hod, and Shamma, and Shilshah, and Ithran, and Beera: Six more sons of Zophah. The eleven-name list (7:36-37) is unusually long, suggesting Zophah's clan held disproportionate weight in Asher's federation — a memory of relative tribal influence preserved in the Chronicler's roll.

7:38 The sons of Jether; Jephunneh, and Pispah, and Ara: A smaller Asherite line. The same name *Jephunneh* belonged to Caleb's father (Num 13:6), but tribal name-sharing is common; this Jephunneh is firmly within Asher's borders.

7:39 The sons of Ulla; Arah, and Haniel, and Rezia: The Asherite catalog closes. *Haniel* (favor of God) was also the name of an Asherite chief at the second-year census (Num 34:23); whether the same person or a namesake, the recurrence shows Asher's families preserving their distinctive names across generations.

7:40 The children of Asher - The rabbins say that the daughters of Asher were very beautiful, and were all matched with kings or priests. Several things relative to the subjects in this chapter may be found explained in the parallel places marked in the margin.
